policy_statement: By 2023, develop a time-of-replacement policy phasing in requirements that newly installed residential and other small-building equipment be efficient and all-electric, customized from simple replacements to full renovations (BO.2-2).
mechanism_description: It phases in requirements so newly installed residential and small-building equipment is efficient and all-electric, reducing on-site fossil fuel use and overall energy consumption to cut building emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building equipment turnover standards → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion + ↑ end-use efficiency → ↓ building energy-related GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Building owners/contractors must install efficient, all-electric appliances/HVAC/water heating at time-of-replacement (and sometimes trigger deeper upgrades during renovations), shifting the building stock away from gas/oil equipment and lowering energy demand.  
2) Mediating process: Electrification removes direct combustion emissions; efficiency reduces delivered energy needed for the same service; upstream emissions depend on grid carbon intensity but generally decline as grids decarbonize.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower annual operational GHG emissions from the building sector (and often lower peak demand/energy use), yielding net mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building operational GHG emissions by replacing fossil-fueled, inefficient equipment with efficient all-electric equipment that cuts on-site combustion and energy use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Equipment replacement requirements drive electrification and efficiency, which directly reduces building operational emissions (primary mitigation effect).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While efficiency can be framed as “Resource Efficiency,” the primary climate-relevant outcome here is explicitly emissions reduction via eliminating fossil combustion and lowering energy-related GHGs; resource savings are instrumental to mitigation rather than the endpoint.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity supply is highly carbon-intensive, near-term emissions benefits may be smaller, but the mechanism still targets operational GHG reductions and typically becomes more beneficial as grids decarbonize; resilience/health co-benefits do not change the primary classification.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy with this mechanism (time-of-replacement electrification + efficiency requirements) is fundamentally aimed at reducing operational building emissions through reduced fossil use and energy demand, so “Mitigation” remains the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., electrification vs efficiency emphasis, residential vs small commercial scope, incentive vs mandate mix) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity with “Resource Efficiency” because efficiency is central; however, because the mechanism’s dominant stated and physical outcome is reduced operational GHG emissions via electrification and reduced fuel combustion, Mitigation is primary. Grid carbon intensity affects magnitude/timing but not pathway direction.
key_indicators: “time-of-replacement policy”; “phases in requirements”; “all newly installed residential and other small building equipment”; “be efficient and all-electric”; “customize requirements for simple equipment replacements to full renovations”
co_benefits: Improved indoor air quality; reduced local combustion-related air pollutants; potential bill savings/comfort improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: While “develop” could imply a planning step, the core mechanism is the creation of enforceable phased-in requirements at time-of-replacement (a mandate rather than an incentive or informational tool).
